story:
Adrian waits on a white shore, letting warm sand stream through his fingers like an hourglass measuring his hope. The waves he once found soothing now torment him. With every crest, he imagines her face might surface—and then it does not. He needs no more gold; his fortune is already vast. He is here only for her.

Years earlier, as a teenager, he had found a mermaid stranded and burning beneath a brutal sun. He cradled her, felt her fear shiver through his arms, and carried her back to the sea. In gratitude, she vowed to make him the richest man alive—to come to this same place each week and bring him treasures lost to the deep. That day, he fell under her spell. Not for the pearls or the promise, but for her smile, and the wonder of her surfacing just for him. He never learned her name. She unmoored his words; he feared that speaking too much might break the fragile bridge between them.

As he grew older, he chose no wife and built no family. He moved to the water’s edge, shaping his home into a shrine for a world that wasn’t his: walls alive with seashell motifs, gardens thick with sea-grass greens, fountains salted to mimic waves. He wanted her to feel she could belong there—should she ever decide to cross the boundary between their worlds.

On this evening, twilight strips the sky of its warmth—and his hope with it. For the first time in decades, she is late. Then, at last, she appears: tail gleaming lavender at dusk, the same ageless beauty she had when they first met, while his own face has hollowed and grayed. She offers him a fistful of gold; he sets it aside without looking. She studies him, and in a voice softened by sorrow says, “This is the last time. My debt is paid.”

The words drop him from a cliff. He can’t speak. When she turns to leave, he stumbles into the surf and cries out for her to stop. She returns to within an arm’s reach, the water lifting and lowering them both. “Don’t,” he says hoarsely. “If you go now, I’ll follow, and the rip current will seize me before I ever see you again.” Gently, she tells him she cannot stay—that she has no place in his world and cannot give him what he longs for. She thanks him for saving her life, but gratitude is not love. She wipes his tears in a final kindness, then slips beneath the waves.

Alone with his ruined hope, Adrian decides to follow. He dives into the dimming water, kicking hard, eyes burning with salt as he searches for even one flash of red hair. A rip current seizes him. The sea spins him; sand scours his skin; kelp tangles his legs; his lungs ache for air. Still he fights, convinced she must be close. Exhaustion and panic close around him. He opens his mouth, and the ocean floods in—heavy as grief, hot as shame.

In that desperate, reckless attempt to hold what he cannot keep, the sea claims him. The fortune he never needed lies forgotten on the shore. The ocean keeps its own treasures—and Adrian, who mistook longing for belonging, yields at last to a world that was never his.
